		Parcel Number:14 00600003029 *Block:--Lot:--Subdivision:--

Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:Undesirable, invasive species. One major scaffold limb is in contact with Tree 2, resulting in a rub condition. The extent of any resulting damage to Tree 1 is not fully visible from the inspection vantage point. Size is approximate.
		
		
			Tree number:2Species:PineTree Size (DBH):27Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:Co-dominant structure is present at both the lower and upper bole areas, with acute unions exhibiting included bark. One primary leader has failed and broken off, with associated decay and wood loss at the former attachment point. Remaining structure includes a dog-legged scaffold branch at the point of former leader failure. A longitudinal fusiform canker is present on the opposing leader, visible from the east side. The canker appears deeply incised with pronounced woundwood formation consistent with a ram’s horn pattern. The defect is located along a gentle bending moment on the tension side of the stem, indicating elevated structural concern at that location. A rub wound is present on the same side of the stem as the canker, located on the south side of the tree and associated with conflict from Tree 1; full extent of the wound is partially obscured. The tree shares a deformed, shared structural root plate with Trees 3 and 4.
		
		
			Tree number:3Species:30Tree Size (DBH):3Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:From a physiological standpoint, the tree appears healthy with no significant canopy decline observed. However, the tree shares a deformed structural root plate with Trees 2 and 4. In the event of approval and subsequent removal of Tree 2, there will be a loss of shared structural root support, resulting in reduced anchorage, increased risk of instability, and increased susceptibility to wind throw due to newly exposed conditions.
		
		
			Tree number:4Species:Water Oak Tree Size (DBH):10Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:Similar to Tree 3, the tree appears physiologically healthy. However, it exhibits poor structural form with a bowed, asymmetrical canopy and a highly suppressed growth habit. The tree shares a deformed, shared structural root plate with Trees 2 and 3. In the event of removal of adjacent tree or trees, there will be a loss of shared structural root support.
		
		
			Tree number:5Species:PineTree Size (DBH):20Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:Tree exhibits an asymmetrical, phototropic canopy with a noticeable lean over the adjacent dwelling’s roof system, resulting in an unbalanced crown. A longitudinal fusiform canker is present on the rear tension side of the stem, located on the west side of the trunk at mid-bole, with visible disruption to the sapwood and pronounced wound development. The location on the tension side, combined with canopy imbalance and lean, indicates elevated structural concern and increased potential for stem failure under load.
		
		
			Tree number:6Species:PineTree Size (DBH):26Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:The tree exhibits a deformed structural root plate in direct association with the adjacent retaining wall. Cavities and decay are present at the base within buttress roots, including open voids. Review of imagery from 2012 to 2014 indicates localized reconstruction of the retaining wall in this area, with mismatched stone patterning and newer mortar suggesting prior disturbance. These conditions indicate compromised root integrity and reduced anchorage at a critical load-bearing zone, with elevated potential for root plate failure. Removal of adjacent larger pines, particularly Trees 2, 3, and 5, would significantly increase site wind exposure and loading on this tree. Observed conditions do not indicate that the tree is well-suited to accommodate this increased exposure.
		
		
			Tree number:7Species:SweetgumTree Size (DBH):39Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:ree appears to have originated as a basal sprout, resulting in a once co-dominant structure. A prior co-dominant leader has been removed or failed, leaving a large wound site with callus development and a small residual cavity that appears partially closed but continues to function as a moisture-collecting point. The lower wound area has developed into a dog-legged form. Upper to mid-bole bark sloughing is present, observed via zoom photography. The underlying cause could not be fully determined during inspection; however, the condition is noted as an additional concern. Percussive sounding with a mallet around the wound site produced hollow tones at locations documented in the photo set, suggesting internal voids or decay. The canopy exhibits an asymmetrical form, resulting in an uneven load distribution directed toward adjacent properties. Targets present within the fall zone include neighboring dwellings and outdoor living areas, including a children’s play area.
		
		
			Tree number:8Species:Waster Oak Tree Size (DBH):28Tree location:See site mapDescription of Tree:Numbered flagging tapeReason for Removal:OtherComments:Upper canopy dieback is present
		
		
			Tree number:9Species:PineTree Size (DBH):29Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:A co-dominant leader structure is present, possibly following loss of the original leader due to a storm-related failure or breakage event. An older leader remnant appears to be present within the co-dominant union. A longitudinal fusiform canker, resembling a “number 11,” is present on the east-northeast side of the stem, located below one of the branch unions.

located in ornamental hedge Reason for Removal:Significant cavity/area of decayComments:A previous pruning cut was made into the trunk wood of the main parent stem, and a seam is visible below this cut. Both of these defects are present below the main canopy load. Like Tree #1, removal is recommended, as alternative mitigation is not sufficient to reduce risk.
		
		
			Tree number:3Species:Red OakTree Size (DBH):39Tree location:Rear right of property Reason for Removal:Significant cavity/area of decayComments:There are two previous pruning cuts on the main parent stem that have not compartmentalized. The cut on the northwest side is showing slime flux and a squirrel infestation. Decay has extended into the trunk wood. Removal is advised, as no mitigation technique will reduce risk to an acceptable level.
		
		
			Tree number:4Species:White OakTree Size (DBH):24Tree location:Rear right of property Reason for Removal:Uncorrectable defectComments:A leader (more than 30% of the canopy) failed on the left side (see photo), and the resulting damage has affected the main parent stem. Because of the extent of canopy loss and stem damage, removal is recommended as the only effective risk mitigation.
		
		
			Tree number:5Species:PineTree Size (DBH):16Tree location:Rear right corner of property Reason for Removal:Uncorrectable defectComments:with a live crown ratio below 30%. The canopy is also heavily weighted toward one side and poses a risk to the target area. Removal is the only recommended action to address the risk.
